rationale_for_baseline_tier: A controllable anterior epistaxis on a clear precipitant, in a haemodynamically stable patient with no anticoagulant use, no bleeding history and no trauma. The correct telehealth disposition is to coach correct anterior pressure now (self-care), give clear time-bound safety-netting, and arrange routine GP review only if it recurs — a T1-level outcome. Escalation is reserved for failure of correct pressure or signs of significant blood loss. Reflexive emergency escalation would be over-triage for a benign, self-limiting bleed.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Bleeding not controlled after 15–20 minutes of correct, sustained anterior pressure
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — after a full 15–20 minutes of correct pressure has failed
escalation_edges.rationale: Failure of correct anterior pressure suggests a posterior source or a bleeding tendency that needs in-person assessment (nasal packing, cautery, ENT review).
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the bleeding hasn't stopped after 15–20 minutes of leaning forward and firmly pinching the soft part of your nose, don't keep waiting — get seen the same day at an urgent care centre or ED, because it may need packing or cautery.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling faint or dizzy on standing, pale and clammy, or losing a large amount of blood
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — go to ED, or call 000 if you feel you might pass out
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate significant blood loss / early hypovolaemia and need emergency assessment; the patient is alone so the threshold is lower.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start feeling faint or dizzy when you stand, go pale and sweaty, or you're losing a lot of blood, go to the emergency department now — call 000 if you feel like you might pass out.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Heavy bleeding pouring down the back of the throat despite pinching the front, bleeding from both nostrils, or bleeding you cannot control at all
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of a posterior bleed, which is not controlled by simple pressure and needs ED/ENT management to protect the airway and stop the haemorrhage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If blood is pouring down the back of your throat even while you pinch the front of your nose, or it's coming from both nostrils and you can't control it, go to the emergency department straight away (000 if it's heavy).

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Repeated nosebleeds over the coming days/weeks, or new unexplained bruising
escalation_edges.rationale: Recurrent bleeds or new bruising may point to an underlying bleeding tendency or a fragile vessel needing cautery — warrants a prompt GP review and possible coagulation screen.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get repeated nosebleeds over the next week or so, or notice new bruising you can't explain, see your GP within a day or two so they can check your clotting and consider referral.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one present to help or monitor
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Ask whether they can get someone to come over or stay on the line; lower the threshold to seek help if the bleeding continues, since there is no one present to assist or call for them if they feel faint.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Right now, sit up and lean forward, firmly pinch the soft part of your nose (just below the hard bridge) and hold it without letting go for 10–15 minutes, breathing through your mouth and spitting out any blood. Most nosebleeds settle with this. Afterwards, avoid blowing or picking your nose, hot showers, hot drinks and heavy exertion for 24 hours, and dab a little petroleum jelly inside the nostril twice a day.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: See your regular GP within about a week if it recurs; call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for after-hours advice; call 000 only if the bleeding is heavy and cannot be controlled or you feel faint.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours: for heavy uncontrolled bleeding or feeling faint call 000 or go to the nearest ED; for advice or a bleed that has settled, call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84).
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: If you do end up at an urgent care centre or ED, keep any discharge summary for your GP, and note how long the bleeding lasted and what stopped it.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the bleed as trivial with no first-aid coaching and no safety-net, missing ongoing blood loss or an unexcluded posterior source.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient keeps using the wrong technique, continues to lose blood while alone, and becomes hypovolaemic before recognising the need for help.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a controllable anterior nosebleed to ED or calling an ambulance without first coaching simple pressure — unnecessary emergency use and avoidable patient alarm.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: A posterior bleeding source and an underlying coagulopathy cannot be visualised or definitively excluded over chat.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Coach correct anterior pressure first and use the response to it as the discriminator, backed by clear time-bound escalation if it fails — conservative but not reflexively emergency.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either falsely reassuring the patient that it is definitely nothing, or over-escalating straight to 000 without first attempting first-line pressure.
